text:
This volume, edited by Pavel Zgaga, Ulrich Teichler, Hans G. Schuetze, and Andra Wolter, offers a comprehensive exploration of the structural and ideological shifts redefining global higher education. Through a diverse range of expert perspectives, the collection examines the tension between local traditions and the push for global connectivity, particularly within the contexts of Europe, Asia, and North America.

The contributing authors analyze the transition from mass education to market-driven systems, the evolving role of the welfare state in university reform, and the impact of "new managerialism" on academic freedom and collegial governance. Specific case studies illuminate the unique trajectories of reform in Japan, China, Mexico, and South-east Europe, while regional analyses of the United States and Canada investigate the rise of private higher education.

A significant portion of the work is dedicated to the social dimensions of academia, including the expansion of lifelong learning, the diversification of the student body, and the pursuit of intergenerational equality. Finally, the collection addresses contemporary policy debates surrounding digital disruption, questioning whether innovations like MOOCs represent a genuine evolution in pedagogy or merely a temporary peak in educational discourse. Together, these essays provide a critical reflection on how institutions navigate the complexities of a rapidly changing world.
